metadata.source_file: Identification of alpha-linolenic acid as a broad-spectrum antiviral against zika, dengue, herpes simplex, influenza virus and SARS-CoV-2 infection.md
metadata.title: Alpha-linolenic acid inhibits Zika virus replication by targeting the viral envelope protein
metadata.abstract: a _Guangdong Provincial Key Laboratory of New Drug Screening, School of Pharmaceutical Sciences, Southern Medical University, Guangzhou, 510515, China_ b _Guangzhou Key Laboratory of Drug Research for Emerging Virus Prevention and Treatment, School of Pharmaceutical Sciences, Southern Medical University, Guangzhou, 510515, China_
metadata.introduction:
Zika virus (ZIKV) is a mosquito-transmitted flavivirus that was initially isolated in Uganda’s Zika forest in 1947 (Dick et al., 1952). ZIKV outbreaks reoccurred between 2015 and 2016, then provoked a public health emergency. Although the acute ZIKV infection usually causes mild symptoms, it was verified to be closely related to serious neurological abnormalities, containing congenital microcephaly in newborn infants and Guillain-Barr´e syndrome in adults (Brasil et al., 2016; de Paula Freitas et al., 2016; Rasmussen et al., 2016). Regrettably, no specific vaccines or antivirals have been approved against ZIKV infection at present. For this reason, we urgently need to exploit effective antivirals with new and accurate targets to combat ZIKV. 

ZIKV contains a single-stranded positive-sense RNA genome of approximately 11 kb in length. The viral genome encodes a polyprotein consisting three structural proteins (C, prM, and E) and seven nonstructural (NS) proteins (NS1, NS2A, NS2B, NS3, NS4A, NS4B, and NS5). NS proteins play roles in genome duplication, polyprotein 

processing, and the evasion of host immune responses (Lee et al., 2021). C protein forms the icosahedral capsid of the virion, supplemented with RNA and surrounded by the globular lipid bilayer (Bhagat et al., 2021). M and E proteins, located on the surface of the virus, constitute the membrane-spanning helices anchored to the cell membrane (DiNunno and Goetschius, 2020). In addition, E protein plays key roles in host cell binding, attachment, and membrane fusion, thus serving as an important target against ZIKV infection (Gao et al., 2019; Lian et al., 2018). 

Alpha-linolenic acid (ALA) is an 18-carbon polyunsaturated ω-3 fatty acid essential for human body; however, it can only be acquired from dietary sources. ALA has been found to have various beneficial functions, including attenuating the risk of stroke and coronary heart disease, improving neurocognitive ability, reducing the obesity rate, and benefiting patients with non-alcoholic fatty liver disease and/or tumors (Blondeau, 2016; Li et al., 2019; Naghshi et al., 2021; Rajna et al., 2018). In addition, ω-3 fatty acids activate immunocytes such as neutrophils, basophils, eosinophils, macrophages, and T and B lymphocytes, thereby enhancing immune functions and stimulating anti-inflammatory 

* Corresponding author. Guangdong Provincial Key Laboratory of New Drug Screening, School of Pharmaceutical Sciences, Southern Medical University, Guangzhou, 510515, China. ** Corresponding author. Guangdong Provincial Key Laboratory of New Drug Screening, School of Pharmaceutical Sciences, Southern Medical University, Guangzhou, 510515, China. 

_E-mail addresses:_ liusw@smu.edu.cn (S. Liu), zoum2000@smu.edu.cn (M. Zou). 

https://doi.org/10.1016/j.antiviral.2023.105666 

Received 2 April 2023; Received in revised form 5 July 2023; Accepted 7 July 2023 Available online 8 July 2023 0166-3542/© 2023 Elsevier B.V. All rights reserved. 

_Antiviral Research 216 (2023) 105666_ 

_Y. Feng et al._ 

responses (Guti´errez et al., 2019). Several ω-3 fatty acids have been shown to inactivate enveloped viruses (Pfaender et al., 2013; Thormar et al., 1987). A recent study revealed that ALA and eicosapentaenoic acid (EPA) showed noticeable efficacy against Severe Acute Respiratory Syndrome Coronavirus 2 (SARS-CoV-2) infection by preventing viral entry (Goc et al., 2021). Nevertheless, little has been reported on the properties of ALA against ZIKV infection so far. Our previous study has shown that human breast milk effectively suppresses ZIKV infection upon storage and the antiviral component exists in the cream (Conzelmann et al., 2019). The polyunsaturated fatty acid ALA is an important component of cream, so it is rational to study its anti-ZIKV activity.

evidence.original_text: Zika virus (ZIKV) is a mosquito-transmitted flavivirus that was initially isolated in Uganda’s Zika forest in 1947 (Dick et al., 1952). ZIKV outbreaks reoccurred between 2015 and 2016, then provoked a public health emergency. Although the acute ZIKV infection usually causes mild symptoms, it was verified to be closely related to serious neurological abnormalities, containing congenital microcephaly in newborn infants and Guillain-Barr´e syndrome in adults (Brasil et al., 2016; de Paula Freitas et al., 2016; Rasmussen et al., 2016). Regrettably, no specific vaccines or antivirals have been approved against ZIKV infection at present. For this reason, we urgently need to exploit effective antivirals with new and accurate targets to combat ZIKV.

Alpha-linolenic acid (ALA) is an 18-carbon polyunsaturated ω-3 fatty acid essential for human body; however, it can only be acquired from dietary sources. ALA has been found to have various beneficial functions, including attenuating the risk of stroke and coronary heart disease, improving neurocognitive ability, reducing the obesity rate, and benefiting patients with non-alcoholic fatty liver disease and/or tumors (Blondeau, 2016; Li et al., 2019; Naghshi et al., 2021; Rajna et al., 2018). In addition, ω-3 fatty acids activate immunocytes such as neutrophils, basophils, eosinophils, macrophages, and T and B lymphocytes, thereby enhancing immune functions and stimulating anti-inflammatory responses (Guti´errez et al., 2019). Several ω-3 fatty acids have been shown to inactivate enveloped viruses (Pfaender et al., 2013; Thormar et al., 1987). A recent study revealed that ALA and eicosapentaenoic acid (EPA) showed noticeable efficacy against Severe Acute Respiratory Syndrome Coronavirus 2 (SARS-CoV-2) infection by preventing viral entry (Goc et al., 2021). Nevertheless, little has been reported on the properties of ALA against ZIKV infection so far.

A recent study revealed that ALA and eicosapentaenoic acid (EPA) showed noticeable efficacy against Severe Acute Respiratory Syndrome Coronavirus 2 (SARS-CoV-2) infection by preventing viral entry (Goc et al., 2021).

entries.normalized: 严重急性呼吸综合征冠状病毒2

evidence.original_text: New Guinea C derivative strain of dengue virus 2 (DENV-2) was a kind gift from Prof. Xiao-guang Chen (School of Public Health, Southern Medical University).
We also explored the activities of ALA against DENV, herpes simplex virus (HSV)-1, influenza virus and SARS-CoV-2.
The influenza A virus (IAV) subtype A/WSN/1933 (H1N1) was kindly supplied by Prof. Zi-feng Yang (State Key Laboratory of Respiratory Diseases, Guangzhou Medical University).

ALA was effective even when its incubation with ZIKV only lasted for 5 min, preventing virus entry by targeting both viruses and cells. Time-of-addition assay revealed that ALA interrupts binding, adsorption, and entry stages of ZIKV replication cycle. It is probably owing to the disruption of viral membrane.

Because ALA interrupts the early stages of ZIKV life cycle, we suppose that it probably targets viral membrane and destroys membrane integrity of the virion. We performed RNase digestion assay using micrococcal nuclease to degrade viral RNA in case of an incomplete envelope. As shown in Fig. 5A, 5–50 μM ALA substantially suppressed ZIKV mRNA loads, indicating a dose-dependent destruction of viral membrane integrity. The positive control (1% Triton) also destroyed the membrane considerably, resulting in a complete RNA degradation by micrococcal nuclease.

To verify the anti-ZIKV capacity of ALA, a set of experiments were performed. Vero cells were plated and cultivated overnight. Serial dilutions of ALA were mixed with ZIKV and the mixture was maintained at room temperature for 10 min. The mixture was then incubated with Vero cells for 1 h. Thereafter, cells were washed and re-fed with fresh medium. At 72 hpi, cells were collected for detection of ZIKV replication at mRNA or protein levels using RT-PCR, cell-based ZIKV immunodetection or western blotting assay. As displayed in Fig. 2A–B, ALA inhibited ZIKV E and NS1 mRNA levels dose-dependently, with an IC50 of 4.29 ± 0.61 μM. The therapeutic index (TI) of ALA was consequently calculated to be 1385. Similarly, we observed in Fig. 2C–D that the expression of ZIKV E protein was inhibited by ALA in a dose-dependent manner. Furthermore, the western blotting test also suggested that ALA inhibited ZIKV E protein expression significantly at concentrations above 25 μM (Fig. 2E–F). The plaque assay and indirect immunofluorescence assay also suggested that ALA inhibited ZIKV progeny virus and cytoplasmic expression of ZIKV E protein (Fig. S1A–S1B). Our initial results indicate that ALA could potentially inhibit ZIKV infection without significant cytotoxicities.

To investigate whether ALA inhibited SARS-CoV-2 infection, we pre-incubated SARS-CoV-2 PsV with serial dilutions of ALA for 30 min at room temperature. Following that, 293T-ACE2 cells were inoculated with the mixture and incubated at 37[◦] C for 12 h. After replacing the supernatant with fresh medium, cells were incubated for another 48 h. Viral infection was determined using the luciferase assay. As shown in Fig. 6F, ALA significantly inhibited SARS-CoV-2 infection, as well as the positive control chloroquine. PNL4/3 blank vector was used as a negative control and little fluorescence was detected (Fig. S4).

We also explored the activities of ALA against DENV, HSV-1, influenza virus and SARS-CoV-2. To investigate whether ALA inhibited DENV infection, we infected BHK21 cells with the pre-incubated ALA-DENV-2 mixture. Following 1 h of incubation at 37[◦] C, the supernatant was replaced with fresh medium. After 48 h of incubation, intracellular protein and RNA were extracted. The protein and mRNA levels of DENV NS5 were determined using western blotting and RT-PCR methods, respectively. As shown in Fig. 6A–B, 15–50 μM ALA prominently reduced the expression of DENV NS5 protein. Similarly, 5–50 μM ALA remarkably reduced transcription of DENV NS5 gene (Fig. 6C). These findings indicate that ALA is an effective inhibitor of DENV-2 infection.

To investigate whether ALA inhibited HSV infection, we infected Vero cells with the pre-incubated ALA-HSV-1 mixture. Following 1 h of incubation at 37[◦] C, the supernatant was replaced with fresh medium. After 24 h of incubation, intracellular protein was extracted and envelope glycoprotein D (gD) was examined using western blotting method. As shown in Fig. 6D and 2.5–10 μM ALA substantially decreased the expression of HSV gD.

To investigate whether ALA inhibited influenza virus infection, we mixed influenza virus A/WSN/1933 (H1N1) with different concentrations of ALA for 10 min at room temperature. MDCK cells were then inoculated with the mixture. After 1 h of incubation at 37[◦] C, the supernatant was replenished with fresh medium containing TPCK and without FBS. Following 48 h of incubation, MTT test was applied to estimate the antiviral activity of ALA. As displayed in Fig. 6E, 12.5–100 μM ALA exerted significant effect on H1N1 infection.

Because ALA interrupts the early stages of ZIKV life cycle, we suppose that it probably targets viral membrane and destroys membrane integrity of the virion. We performed RNase digestion assay using micrococcal nuclease to degrade viral RNA in case of an incomplete envelope. As shown in Fig. 5A, 5–50 μM ALA substantially suppressed ZIKV mRNA loads, indicating a dose-dependent destruction of viral membrane integrity. The positive control (1% Triton) also destroyed the membrane considerably, resulting in a complete RNA degradation by micrococcal nuclease.

ALA was effective even when its incubation with ZIKV only lasted for 5 min, preventing virus entry by targeting both viruses and cells. Time-of-addition assay revealed that ALA interrupts binding, adsorption, and entry stages of ZIKV replication cycle. It is probably owing to the disruption of viral membrane.

To verify the anti-ZIKV capacity of ALA, a set of experiments were performed. Vero cells were plated and cultivated overnight. Serial dilutions of ALA were mixed with ZIKV and the mixture was maintained at room temperature for 10 min. The mixture was then incubated with Vero cells for 1 h. Thereafter, cells were washed and re-fed with fresh medium. At 72 hpi, cells were collected for detection of ZIKV replication at mRNA or protein levels using RT-PCR, cell-based ZIKV immunodetection or western blotting assay. As displayed in Fig. 2A–B, ALA inhibited ZIKV E and NS1 mRNA levels dose-dependently, with an IC50 of 4.29 ± 0.61 μM. The therapeutic index (TI) of ALA was consequently calculated to be 1385. Similarly, we observed in Fig. 2C–D that the expression of ZIKV E protein was inhibited by ALA in a dose-dependent manner. Furthermore, the western blotting test also suggested that ALA inhibited ZIKV E protein expression significantly at concentrations above 25 μM (Fig. 2E–F). The plaque assay and indirect immunofluorescence assay also suggested that ALA inhibited ZIKV progeny virus and cytoplasmic expression of ZIKV E protein (Fig. S1A–S1B). Our initial results indicate that ALA could potentially inhibit ZIKV infection without significant cytotoxicities.

Next, we assessed how fast ALA exerted its inhibitory effect. ZIKV was incubated with or without 40 μM ALA for a specific period, which ranged from 5 to 60 min. Vero cells were then inoculated with the mixture. After 1 h, the inoculum was replaced by fresh medium. At 72 hpi, cells were collected for ZIKV E mRNA detection by RT-PCR, as well as ZIKV E protein detection by western blotting assay. ALA treatment caused a 99% reduction rate of E mRNA level, even if the mixture was incubated for only 5 min (Fig. 3A). Similarly, ZIKV E protein expression also decreased quickly as shown in Fig. 3C and D. Therefore, we may conclude that ALA inhibits ZIKV replication quickly. Time-of-addition assay was performed to investigate the antiviral mechanism of ALA. The compound was added to Vero cells for 30 min before infection, or mixed with ZIKV for 0, 10 and 30 min before infection, or added to Vero cells at 0, 1 and 4 h after 1 h of ZIKV infection. Cells were collected 24 hpi and transcription of ZIKV E gene was detected using RT-PCR. As shown in Fig. 3B, ALA exhibited significant inhibitory effect when it was either pre-incubated with Vero cells for 30 min or pre-incubated with ZIKV for 0–30 min before infection. However, while the cells were already incubated with ZIKV for 1 h, addition of ALA showed insignificant antiviral activities even when the incubation time lasted for 4 h, indicating ALA had no effect on the entered viruses. Thus, ALA probably targets both viruses and cells, preventing virus entry.

To clarify which stages of ZIKV lifecycle was blocked by ALA, we firstly performed the virus binding test. Vero cells were seeded in 6-well plates and cultivated overnight. After that, diluted ALA was incubated with ZIKV at 37°C for 10 min before inoculation to the cell culture. Epigallocatechin gallate (EGCG) was adopted as a positive control. The supernatant was discarded after 1 h of incubation at 4°C, which allowed virus binding rather than internalization, and cells were then washed five times with PBS. Cellular RNA was isolated, followed by RT-qPCR test for quantification of ZIKV mRNA copies. The mRNA levels were significantly reduced when cells were treated with 5–50 μM ALA (Fig. 4A) and 10 μM EGCG (data not shown). These results indicate that ALA inhibits ZIKV binding to Vero cells. We then tested the anti-adsorption activity of ALA. Similar to the binding test, Vero cells were inoculated with ALA-ZIKV mixture and incubated at 4°C for 1 h. Cells were then washed with PBS or treated with pronase, and then re-fed with fresh medium. Following 72 h of incubation, cellular RNA was isolated and RT-PCR test was conducted for quantification of ZIKV E and NS1 mRNA copies. As shown in Fig. 4B–C, in PBS washed cells, ALA at 5–50 μM remarkably prevented ZIKV infection, indicating that ALA inhibits ZIKV adsorption to Vero cells. While pronase was used to remove the cell-bound but uninternalized virus particles, similar results were obtained (Fig. S2A–S2B). Finally, we performed the anti-entry experiment. Vero cells were inoculated with ZIKV and incubated at 4°C for 1 h. The supernatant was discarded. Cells were then washed with PBS and exposed to fresh medium with ALA at different concentrations. After 1 h of incubation at 37°C, the culture was replenished with fresh medium. Following 72 hpi incubation, cellular RNA was isolated and RT-PCR test was conducted for quantification of ZIKV E and NS1 mRNA copies. As shown in Fig. 4D–E, ALA at 15–50 μM remarkably prevented ZIKV infection, indicating that ALA inhibits ZIKV entry into Vero cells. In conclusion, ALA interrupts the early stages in ZIKV replication cycle, including virus binding, adsorption and entry.

Because ALA interrupts the early stages of ZIKV life cycle, we suppose that it probably targets viral membrane and destroys membrane integrity of the virion. We performed RNase digestion assay using micrococcal nuclease to degrade viral RNA in case of an incomplete envelope. As shown in Fig. 5A, 5–50 μM ALA substantially suppressed ZIKV mRNA loads, indicating a dose-dependent destruction of viral membrane integrity. The positive control (1% Triton) also destroyed the membrane considerably, resulting in a complete RNA degradation by micrococcal nuclease.

We also explored the activities of ALA against DENV, HSV-1, influenza virus and SARS-CoV-2. To investigate whether ALA inhibited DENV infection, we infected BHK21 cells with the pre-incubated ALA-DENV-2 mixture. Following 1 h of incubation at 37°C, the supernatant was replaced with fresh medium. After 48 h of incubation, intracellular protein and RNA were extracted. The protein and mRNA levels of DENV NS5 were determined using western blotting and RT-PCR methods, respectively. As shown in Fig. 6A–B, 15–50 μM ALA prominently reduced the expression of DENV NS5 protein. Similarly, 5–50 μM ALA remarkably reduced transcription of DENV NS5 gene (Fig. 6C). These findings indicate that ALA is an effective inhibitor of DENV-2 infection. To investigate whether ALA inhibited HSV infection, we infected Vero cells with the pre-incubated ALA-HSV-1 mixture. Following 1 h of incubation at 37°C, the supernatant was replaced with fresh medium. After 24 h of incubation, intracellular protein was extracted and envelope glycoprotein D (gD) was examined using western blotting method. As shown in Fig. 6D and 2.5–10 μM ALA substantially decreased the expression of HSV gD. To investigate whether ALA inhibited influenza virus infection, we mixed influenza virus A/WSN/1933 (H1N1) with different concentrations of ALA for 10 min at room temperature. MDCK cells were then inoculated with the mixture. After 1 h of incubation at 37°C, the supernatant was replenished with fresh medium containing TPCK and without FBS. Following 48 h of incubation, MTT test was applied to estimate the antiviral activity of ALA. As displayed in Fig. 6E, 12.5–100 μM ALA exerted significant effect on H1N1 infection. To investigate whether ALA inhibited SARS-CoV-2 infection, we pre-incubated SARS-CoV-2 PsV with serial dilutions of ALA for 30 min at room temperature. Following that, 293T-ACE2 cells were inoculated with the mixture and incubated at 37°C for 12 h. After replacing the supernatant with fresh medium, cells were incubated for another 48 h. Viral infection was determined using the luciferase assay. As shown in Fig. 6F, ALA significantly inhibited SARS-CoV-2 infection, as well as the positive control chloroquine. PNL4/3 blank vector was used as a negative control and little fluorescence was detected (Fig. S4).

Vero (African green monkey kidney cell line), MDCK (Madin-Daby canine kidney cell line), BHK21 (Baby hamster Syrian kidney cell line), and HEK-293T (Human embryonic kidney cell line) cells were grown in Dulbecco’s Modified Eagle’s Medium (DMEM, Gibco, USA) containing 10% fetal bovine serum (FBS, ExCell Bio, China) and 1% penicillin/ streptomycin (Invitrogen, USA). HEK-293T cells expressing human angiotensin-converting enzyme (ACE) 2 (293T-ACE2) were introduced before (Yang et al., 2021) and cultured with 2 μg/mL puromycin (Invitrogen, USA). ZIKV clinical isolate z16006 (GenBank accession number, KU955589.1) was kindly donated by Prof. Chang-wen Ke (Guangdong Provincial Center for Disease Control and Prevention). New Guinea C derivative strain of dengue virus 2 (DENV-2) was a kind gift from Prof. Xiao-guang Chen (School of Public Health, Southern Medical University). The influenza A virus (IAV) subtype A/WSN/1933 (H1N1) was kindly supplied by Prof. Zi-feng Yang (State Key Laboratory of Respiratory Diseases, Guangzhou Medical University). The plasmid encoding SARS-CoV-2 S protein (pcDNA3.1-SARS-CoV-2 S) was represented formerly (Yang et al., 2021). The empty luciferase reporter vec− tor, pNL4-3.Luc.R-E , was conserved in our laboratory. ALA (Acmec Biochemical, China) was diluted in dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO) to 10 mM and preserved at − 20°C.

entries.description: Standardized antiviral assay protocol for ZIKV, DENV, HSV-1, influenza virus, and SARS-CoV-2

evidence.original_text: The 3-(4, 5-dimethylthiazolyl-2)-2, 5-diphenyltetrazolium bromide (MTT) assay was applied to assess the effect of ALA on the metabolic activities of Vero, MDCK and BHK21 cells. Briefly, cells were exposed to serially diluted ALA for a certain time and subsequently treated with 0.5 mg/mL MTT. After 4 h, the supernatant was discarded and the purple crystals in viable cells were solubilized in dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO) for 20 min. The absorbance values (λ = 570 nm) were measured using a microplate reader (Tecan, Switzerland) and the cell viabilities were calculated by comparing with the untreated cells.

entries.description: Standardized cytotoxicity assessment protocol using MTT assay

evidence.original_text: Titers of virus stocks were determined by median tissue culture infectious dose (TCID50) endpoint titration. Vero cells were plated in 96well culture plates at 1 × 10^4 cells per well for 12 h. Serially diluted virus stocks were used to infect the cells, leading to the final attenuations from 1:10^1 to 1:10^8. After incubation for 5–7 days, the cytopathic effect was monitored every day. TCID50 values were determined following the methods described by Reed and Muench (Reed and Muench, 1938).

entries.description: Standardized virus titration protocol using TCID50 endpoint titration

evidence.original_text: The cell-based ZIKV immunodetection assay was executed to measure ZIKV NS1 protein levels as previously described (Zou et al., 2020). Four days post ZIKV infection (100TCID50), the supernatant was discarded, and the cells were washed with phosphate-buffered saline (PBS), then fixed with 4% paraformaldehyde (PFA) at 25°C. After 20 min, the cells were treated with 100% precooled methanol at 4°C for 5 min and washed with PBS. Next, the cells were blocked with 2% skim milk in PBS at 4°C for 1 h and washed three times using 0.3% (v/v) Tween 20 in PBS. Then, the cells were soaked in the primary ZIKV E or NS1 antibody (BioFront Technologies, USA) (1:1000) at 37°C for 1 h, followed by the horseradish peroxidase (HRP)-labeled secondary antibody (Cell Signaling Technology, USA) (1:2000). Thereafter, the cells were washed five times with washing buffer and overlaid with tetramethylbenzidine (TMB) peroxidase substrate for 5 min in dark area. Finally, 1 mM H2SO4 was added to the wells to stop the reaction and the optical density at 450 nm was detected using a GeniosPro microplate reader (Tecan, USA).

entries.description: Standardized cell-based ZIKV immunodetection assay protocol

evidence.original_text: Ribonucleic acid (RNA) was extracted from cell lysates using a viral RNA extraction kit (Promega, USA) according to the manufacturer’s protocols. The complementary deoxyribonucleic acid (cDNA) was reverse-transcribed using the SuperScript-III kit (Takara, Japan). An RT-PCR assay was performed using the LightCycler® 480 (Roche, USA) system with an SYBR Green Ab Taq qPCR Mix (Beyotime, China). Relative ZIKV RNA copies were determined using the 2−ΔΔCT method and the data were normalized to the glyceraldehyde-3-phosphate dehydrogenase (GAPDH) gene. The designed primer sequences are listed in Table 1. RT-qPCR was performed according to previous study (Hui et al., 2018). RNA was extracted and reverse-transcribed using the same method as RT-PCR. Tenfold dilutions of ZIKV plasmid standards (10^0-10^9 copies/μL) were prepared. ZIKV RNA copies were determined using the 2−ΔΔCT method and quantified according to the standard curve. The designed primer sequences are listed in Table 2.

entries.description: Standardized RT-PCR and RT-qPCR protocols for viral RNA quantification

evidence.original_text: Cells were scraped and lysed with radioimmunoprecipitation assay (RIPA) buffer and the total protein concentrations were analyzed using a bicinchoninic acid (BCA) kit (KeyGEN, China). Protein samples were separated using 10% sodium dodecyl sulfate-polyacrylamide gel electrophoresis (SDS-PAGE) and electro-transferred onto a polyvinylidene difluoride (PVDF) membrane (Millipore, USA). The membrane was blocked for 1 h with tris-buffered saline with tween 20 (TBST) solution containing 5% slim milk and subsequently treated with the primary antibodies at 4°C overnight. HRP-labeled IgG was used as the secondary antibody. The protein blots were then developed with enhanced chemiluminescence (ECL) reagents and detected with the FluorChem R imager (ProteinSimple, USA).

entries.description: Standardized western blotting protocol for protein expression analysis

evidence.original_text: Anti-binding assay: Vero cells were plated in 12-well culture plates and incubated for 12 h. ZIKV was mixed with or without ALA for 10 min at 37°C. Then the mixture was inoculated into the plates (MOI = 2.5) and incubated for 1 h at 4°C. Afterward, the cells were washed with PBS and collected for viral RNA load detection using the RT-qPCR method. Anti-adsorption assay: Vero cells were plated in 12-well culture plates and incubated for 12 h. ZIKV was mixed with ALA or fresh medium (v/v, 1:1) before being used to infect the cells (100TCID50). Uncombined viruses were removed by washing with PBS or treating with pronase an hour after infection at 4°C. Then fresh medium was replenished and the incubation continued for 72 h at 37°C. Total intracellular viral RNA was quantified using RT-PCR method. Anti-entry assay: Vero cells were plated in 12-well culture plates and incubated for 12 h. The cells were then inoculated with ZIKV (100TCID50) and incubated for 1 h at 4°C. Uncombined viruses were removed by washing with PBS. Following that, fresh medium consisting of serially diluted ALA was added. At the end of 1 h incubation at 37°C, the cells were rinsed once more with PBS and re-fed with fresh medium. Following 72 h of incubation at 37°C, total RNA was isolated from cells and the viral RNA load was quantified using RT-PCR method.

entries.description: Standardized antiviral assay protocols for binding, adsorption, and entry inhibition

evidence.original_text: RNase digestion assay: Serial dilutions of ALA were mixed with ZIKV (1000 PFU). After 2 h of incubation at 37°C, RNA leaked from the virion was degraded by micrococcal nuclease (NEB, USA) for 1 h at 37°C (Xiong et al., 2021). RNA was isolated and quantified using RT-qPCR method.

entries.description: Standardized RNase digestion assay protocol for virion integrity assessment

evidence.original_text: Inhibition of influenza A virus infection assay: Influenza A virus was incubated with different concentrations of ALA for 30 min. MDCK cells were infected with the mixture (100 TCID50) in 96-well plates at 37°C for 1 h. Then the supernatant was replaced by fresh medium supplemented with 0.6 μg/mL L-1-tosylamido-2-phenylethyl chloromethyl ketone (TPCK) (Sigma-Aldrich, MO, USA) and without FBS. At 48 hpi, the antiviral activity was detected by MTT-based assay.

entries.description: Standardized influenza A virus inhibition assay protocol

evidence.original_text: Inhibition of SARS-CoV-2 pseudovirus (PsV) infection assay: Both pNL4-3.Luc.R-E and pcDNA3.1-SARS-CoV-2 S plasmids were co-transfected into HEK-293T cells using PolyJet (SignaGen, USA). After 48 h of incubation, the supernatant containing the released PsV was collected and stored at −80°C after centrifugation for 10 min at 2000×g. TCID50 was quantified in accordance with the methods described by Reed and Muench (Reed and Muench, 1938). To investigate the antiviral effect of ALA on SARS-CoV-2 infection, 293T-ACE2 cells were plated in 96-well plates and incubated for 12 h. SARS-CoV-2 PsV stock solutions were exposed to different concentrations of ALA for 30 min at 25°C, then transferred to cell cultures. At 12 hpi, the supernatant was discarded and replaced with fresh medium. After another 48 h of incubation, cells were accumulated and the luciferase activity was detected by recording luminescence on a fluorescence reader (Synergy HTX, BioTek, USA) after mixing with the luciferase assay substrate (Promega, USA).

entries.description: Standardized SARS-CoV-2 pseudovirus infection inhibition assay protocol

evidence.original_text: All statistical analyses were conducted using GraphPad Prism 8.0. ALA concentrations inhibiting viral replication by 50% (IC50) and those reducing cellular metabolism by 50% (CC50) were obtained using fourparametric nonlinear regression and shown as mean ± standard deviation (SD). One-way analysis of variance and Bonferroni’s multiple comparison test were applied for comparison. Significant difference was defined as p < 0.05.

The 3-(4, 5-dimethylthiazolyl-2)-2, 5-diphenyltetrazolium bromide (MTT) assay was applied to assess the effect of ALA on the metabolic activities of Vero, MDCK and BHK21 cells. Briefly, cells were exposed to serially diluted ALA for a certain time and subsequently treated with 0.5 mg/mL MTT. After 4 h, the supernatant was discarded and the purple crystals in viable cells were solubilized in dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO) for 20 min. The absorbance values (λ = 570 nm) were measured using a microplate reader (Tecan, Switzerland) and the cell viabilities were calculated by comparing with the untreated cells.

Titers of virus stocks were determined by median tissue culture infectious dose (TCID50) endpoint titration. Vero cells were plated in 96well culture plates at 1 × 10[4 ] cells per well for 12 h. Serially diluted virus stocks were used to infect the cells, leading to the final attenuations from 1:10[1 ] to 1:10[8] . After incubation for 5–7 days, the cytopathic effect was monitored every day. TCID50 values were determined following the methods described by Reed and Muench (Reed and Muench, 1938).

The cell-based ZIKV immunodetection assay was executed to measure ZIKV NS1 protein levels as previously described (Zou et al., 2020). Four days post ZIKV infection (100TCID50), the supernatant was discarded, and the cells were washed with phosphate-buffered saline (PBS), then fixed with 4% paraformaldehyde (PFA) at 25[◦] C. After 20 min, the cells were treated with 100% precooled methanol at 4[◦] C for 5 min and washed with PBS. Next, the cells were blocked with 2% skim milk in PBS at 4[◦] C for 1 h and washed three times using 0.3% (v/v) Tween 20 in PBS. Then, the cells were soaked in the primary ZIKV E or NS1 antibody (BioFront Technologies, USA) (1:1000) at 37[◦] C for, followed by the horseradish peroxidase (HRP)-labeled secondary antibody (Cell Signaling Technology, USA) (1:2000). Thereafter, the cells were washed five times with washing buffer and overlaid with tetramethylbenzidine (TMB) peroxidase substrate for 5 min in dark area. Finally, 1 mM H2SO4 was added to the wells to stop the reaction and the optical density at 450 nm was detected using a GeniosPro microplate reader (Tecan, USA).

Ribonucleic acid (RNA) was extracted from cell lysates using a viral RNA extraction kit (Promega, USA) according to the manufacturer’s protocols. The complementary deoxyribonucleic acid (cDNA) was reverse-transcribed using the SuperScript-III kit (Takara, Japan). An RT-PCR assay was performed using the LightCycler® 480 (Roche, USA) system with an SYBR Green Ab Taq qPCR Mix (Beyotime, China). Relative ZIKV RNA copies were determined using the 2[−][ΔΔ][CT ] method and the data were normalized to the glyceraldehyde-3-phosphate dehydrogenase (GAPDH) gene. The designed primer sequences are listed in Table 1.

RT-qPCR was performed according to previous study (Hui et al., 2018). RNA was extracted and reverse-transcribed using the same method as RT-PCR. Tenfold dilutions of ZIKV plasmid standards (10[◦] -10[9] copies/μL) were prepared. ZIKV RNA copies were determined using the 2[−][ΔΔ][CT ] method and quantified according to the standard curve. The designed primer sequences are listed in Table 2.

Cells were scraped and lysed with radioimmunoprecipitation assay (RIPA) buffer and the total protein concentrations were analyzed using a bicinchoninic acid (BCA) kit (KeyGEN, China). Protein samples were separated using 10% sodium dodecyl sulfate-polyacrylamide gel electrophoresis (SDS-PAGE) and electro-transferred onto a polyvinylidene difluoride (PVDF) membrane (Millipore, USA). The membrane was blocked for 1 h with tris-buffered saline with tween 20 (TBST) solution containing 5% slim milk and subsequently treated with the primary antibodies at 4[◦] C overnight. HRP-labeled IgG was used as the secondary antibody. The protein blots were then developed with enhanced chemiluminescence (ECL) reagents and detected with the FluorChem R imager (ProteinSimple, USA).

The 3-(4, 5-dimethylthiazolyl-2)-2, 5-diphenyltetrazolium bromide (MTT) assay was applied to assess the effect of ALA on the metabolic activities of Vero, MDCK and BHK21 cells. Briefly, cells were exposed to serially diluted ALA for a certain time and subsequently treated with 0.5 mg/mL MTT. After 4 h, the supernatant was discarded and the purple crystals in viable cells were solubilized in dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO) for 20 min. The absorbance values (λ = 570 nm) were measured using a microplate reader (Tecan, Switzerland) and the cell viabilities were calculated by comparing with the untreated cells.

entries.setup: MTT assay on Vero, MDCK, and BHK21 cells

evidence.original_text: Vero cells were plated in 12-well culture plates and incubated for 12 h. ZIKV was mixed with or without ALA for 10 min at 37[◦] C. Then the mixture was inoculated into the plates (MOI = 2.5) and incubated for 1 h at 4[◦] C. Afterward, the cells were washed with PBS and collected for viral RNA load detection using the RT-qPCR method.

entries.setup: Vero cells incubated with pre-incubated mixture of ALA and ZIKV at 4°C

evidence.original_text: Vero cells were plated in 12-well culture plates and incubated for 12 h. ZIKV was mixed with ALA or fresh medium (v/v, 1:1) before being used to infect the cells (100TCID50). Uncombined viruses were removed by washing with PBS or treating with pronase an hour after infection at 4[◦] C. Then fresh medium was replenished and the incubation continued for 72 h at 37[◦] C. Total intracellular viral RNA was quantified using RT-PCR method.

entries.setup: Vero cells inoculated with pre-incubated mixture of ALA and ZIKV at 4°C

evidence.original_text: Vero cells were plated in 12-well culture plates and incubated for 12 h. The cells were then inoculated with ZIKV (100TCID50) and incubated for 1 h at 4[◦] C. Uncombined viruses were removed by washing with PBS. Following that, fresh medium consisting of serially diluted ALA was added. At the end of 1 h incubation at 37[◦] C, the cells were rinsed once more with PBS and re-fed with fresh medium. Following 72 h of incubation at 37[◦] C, total RNA was isolated from cells and the viral RNA load was quantified using RT-PCR method.

entries.setup: Vero cells inoculated with ZIKV at 4°C, then treated with ALA at 37°C

evidence.original_text: We conducted the time-of-addition experiment at an MOI of 0.5 to investigate which stages of ZIKV life cycle was impacted by 40 μM ALA. Cells were treated with ALA 0.5 h before virus infection, or with virusALA mixture preincubated for 0, 10, 30 min, or with ALA at 0, 1, 4 h after 1 h infection, and ultimately cells were incubated for 24 hpi. Following that, cells were harvested and cellular vRNA was quantified using RT-PCR method.

entries.setup: Vero cells treated with ALA at different stages of ZIKV infection

evidence.original_text: Serial dilutions of ALA were mixed with ZIKV (1000 PFU). After 2 h of incubation at 37[◦] C, RNA leaked from the virion was degraded by micrococcal nuclease (NEB, USA) for 1 h at 37[◦] C (Xiong et al., 2021). RNA was isolated and quantified using RT-qPCR method.

entries.setup: ALA-treated ZIKV digested by micrococcal nuclease

evidence.original_text: Influenza A virus was incubated with different concentrations of ALA for 30 min. MDCK cells were infected with the mixture (100 TCID50) in 96-well plates at 37[◦] C for 1 h. Then the supernatant was replaced by fresh medium supplemented with 0.6 μg/mL L-1-tosylamido-2-phenylethyl chloromethyl ketone (TPCK) (Sigma-Aldrich, MO, USA) and without FBS. At 48 hpi, the antiviral activity was detected by MTT-based assay.

entries.task: inhibition of influenza A virus infection assay
entries.setup: MDCK cells infected with pre-incubated mixture of ALA and influenza virus

evidence.original_text: Both pNL4-3.Luc.R-E and pcDNA3.1-SARS-CoV-2 S plasmids were co-transfected into HEK-293T cells using PolyJet (SignaGen, USA). After 48 h of incubation, the supernatant containing the released PsV was collected and stored at − 80[◦] C after centrifugation for 10 min at 2000× g . TCID50 was quantified in accordance with the methods described by Reed and Muench (Reed and Muench, 1938). To investigate the antiviral effect of ALA on SARS-CoV-2 infection, 293T-ACE2 cells were plated in 96-well plates and incubated for 12 h. SARS-CoV-2 PsV stock solutions were exposed to different concentrations of ALA for 30 min at 25[◦] C, then transferred to cell cultures. At 12 hpi, the supernatant was discarded and replaced with fresh medium. After another 48 h of incubation, cells were accumulated and the luciferase activity was detected by recording luminescence on a fluorescence reader (Synergy HTX, BioTek, USA) after mixing with the luciferase assay substrate (Promega, USA).

entries.task: inhibition of SARS-CoV-2 pseudovirus (PsV) infection assay
entries.setup: 293T-ACE2 cells infected with pre-incubated mixture of ALA and SARS-CoV-2 pseudovirus

evidence.original_text: MTT assay. Cells were treated with various concentrations of ALA to determine CC50. CC50 after treating Vero cells with ALA was found to be 5.94 ± 0.67 mM (Fig. 1A–B). The maximal non-toxic concentration was defined as 2.25 mM to avoid the interference of virus-induced cytopathic effect. ALA was not toxic to MDCK and BHK21 cells at concentrations lower than or equal to 1 mM (Fig. 1C–D).

entries.context: ALA on Vero cells determined by MTT assay

evidence.original_text: To verify the anti-ZIKV capacity of ALA, a set of experiments were performed. Vero cells were plated and cultivated overnight. Serial dilutions of ALA were mixed with ZIKV and the mixture was maintained at room temperature for 10 min. The mixture was then incubated with Vero cells for 1 h. Thereafter, cells were washed and re-fed with fresh medium. At 72 hpi, cells were collected for detection of ZIKV replication at mRNA or protein levels using RT-PCR, cell-based ZIKV immunodetection or western blotting assay. As displayed in Fig. 2A–B, ALA inhibited ZIKV E and NS1 mRNA levels dose-dependently, with an IC50 of 4.29 ± 0.61 μM. The therapeutic index (TI) of ALA was consequently calculated to be 1385.

entries.context: ALA inhibiting ZIKV E and NS1 mRNA levels in Vero cells

evidence.original_text: To verify the anti-ZIKV capacity of ALA, a set of experiments were performed. Vero cells were plated and cultivated overnight. Serial dilutions of ALA were mixed with ZIKV and the mixture was maintained at room temperature for 10 min. The mixture was then incubated with Vero cells for 1 h. Thereafter, cells were washed and re-fed with fresh medium. At 72 hpi, cells were collected for detection of ZIKV replication at mRNA or protein levels using RT-PCR, cell-based ZIKV immunodetection or western blotting assay. As displayed in Fig. 2A–B, ALA inhibited ZIKV E and NS1 mRNA levels dose-dependently, with an IC50 of 4.29 ± 0.61 μM. The therapeutic index (TI) of ALA was consequently calculated to be 1385.

Next, we assessed how fast ALA exerted its inhibitory effect. ZIKV was incubated with or without 40 μM ALA for a specific period, which ranged from 5 to 60 min. Vero cells were then inoculated with the mixture. After 1 h, the inoculum was replaced by fresh medium. At 72 hpi, cells were collected for ZIKV E mRNA detection by RT-PCR, as well as ZIKV E protein detection by western blotting assay. ALA treatment caused a 99% reduction rate of E mRNA level, even if the mixture was incubated for only 5 min (Fig. 3A). Similarly, ZIKV E protein expression also decreased quickly as shown in Fig. 3C and D. Therefore, we may conclude that ALA inhibits ZIKV replication quickly.

entries.context: ALA (40 μM) inhibiting ZIKV E mRNA level after 5 min pre-incubation

evidence.original_text: MTT assay. Cells were treated with various concentrations of ALA to determine CC50. CC50 after treating Vero cells with ALA was found to be 5.94 ± 0.67 mM (Fig. 1A–B). The maximal non-toxic concentration was defined as 2.25 mM to avoid the interference of virus-induced cytopathic effect. ALA was not toxic to MDCK and BHK21 cells at concentrations lower than or equal to 1 mM (Fig. 1C–D).

We also explored the activities of ALA against DENV, HSV-1, influenza virus and SARS-CoV-2. To investigate whether ALA inhibited DENV infection, we infected BHK21 cells with the pre-incubated ALA-DENV-2 mixture. Following 1 h of incubation at 37◦ C, the supernatant was replaced with fresh medium. After 48 h of incubation, intracellular protein and RNA were extracted. The protein and mRNA levels of DENV NS5 were determined using western blotting and RT-PCR methods, respectively. As shown in Fig. 6A–B, 15–50 μM ALA prominently reduced the expression of DENV NS5 protein. Similarly, 5–50 μM ALA remarkably reduced transcription of DENV NS5 gene (Fig. 6C). These findings indicate that ALA is an effective inhibitor of DENV-2 infection. To investigate whether ALA inhibited HSV infection, we infected Vero cells with the pre-incubated ALA-HSV-1 mixture. Following 1 h of incubation at 37◦ C, the supernatant was replaced with fresh medium. After 24 h of incubation, intracellular protein was extracted and envelope glycoprotein D (gD) was examined using western blotting method. As shown in Fig. 6D and 2.5–10 μM ALA substantially decreased the expression of HSV gD. To investigate whether ALA inhibited influenza virus infection, we mixed influenza virus A/WSN/1933 (H1N1) with different concentrations of ALA for 10 min at room temperature. MDCK cells were then inoculated with the mixture. After 1 h of incubation at 37◦ C, the supernatant was replenished with fresh medium containing TPCK and without FBS. Following 48 h of incubation, MTT test was applied to estimate the antiviral activity of ALA. As displayed in Fig. 6E, 12.5–100 μM ALA exerted significant effect on H1N1 infection. To investigate whether ALA inhibited SARS-CoV-2 infection, we pre-incubated SARS-CoV-2 PsV with serial dilutions of ALA for 30 min at room temperature. Following that, 293T-ACE2 cells were inoculated with the mixture and incubated at 37◦ C for 12 h. After replacing the supernatant with fresh medium, cells were incubated for another 48 h. Viral infection was determined using the luciferase assay. As shown in Fig. 6F, ALA significantly inhibited SARS-CoV-2 infection, as well as the positive control chloroquine. PNL4/3 blank vector was used as a negative control and little fluorescence was detected (Fig. S4).

Alpha-linolenic acid (ALA) is an 18-carbon polyunsaturated ω-3 fatty acid essential for human body; however, it can only be acquired from dietary sources. ALA has been found to have various beneficial functions, including attenuating the risk of stroke and coronary heart disease, improving neurocognitive ability, reducing the obesity rate, and benefiting patients with non-alcoholic fatty liver disease and/or tumors (Blondeau, 2016; Li et al., 2019; Naghshi et al., 2021; Rajna et al., 2018). In addition, ω-3 fatty acids activate immunocytes such as neutrophils, basophils, eosinophils, macrophages, and T and B lymphocytes, thereby enhancing immune functions and stimulating anti-inflammatory responses (Guti´errez et al., 2019). Several ω-3 fatty acids have been shown to inactivate enveloped viruses (Pfaender et al., 2013; Thormar et al., 1987). A recent study revealed that ALA and eicosapentaenoic acid (EPA) showed noticeable efficacy against Severe Acute Respiratory Syndrome Coronavirus 2 (SARS-CoV-2) infection by preventing viral entry (Goc et al., 2021). Nevertheless, little has been reported on the properties of ALA against ZIKV infection so far. Our previous study has shown that human breast milk effectively suppresses ZIKV infection upon storage and the antiviral component exists in the cream (Conzelmann et al., 2019). The polyunsaturated fatty acid ALA is an important component of cream, so it is rational to study its anti-ZIKV activity.

To verify the anti-ZIKV capacity of ALA, a set of experiments were performed. Vero cells were plated and cultivated overnight. Serial dilutions of ALA were mixed with ZIKV and the mixture was maintained at room temperature for 10 min. The mixture was then incubated with Vero cells for 1 h. Thereafter, cells were washed and re-fed with fresh medium. At 72 hpi, cells were collected for detection of ZIKV replication at mRNA or protein levels using RT-PCR, cell-based ZIKV immunodetection or western blotting assay. As displayed in Fig. 2A–B, ALA inhibited ZIKV E and NS1 mRNA levels dose-dependently, with an IC50 of 4.29 ± 0.61 μM. The therapeutic index (TI) of ALA was consequently calculated to be 1385. Similarly, we observed in Fig. 2C–D that the expression of ZIKV E protein was inhibited by ALA in a dose-dependent manner. Furthermore, the western blotting test also suggested that ALA inhibited ZIKV E protein expression significantly at concentrations above 25 μM (Fig. 2E–F). The plaque assay and indirect immunofluorescence assay also suggested that ALA inhibited ZIKV progeny virus and cytoplasmic expression of ZIKV E protein (Fig. S1A–S1B). Our initial results indicate that ALA could potentially inhibit ZIKV infection without significant cytotoxicities.

Next, we assessed how fast ALA exerted its inhibitory effect. ZIKV was incubated with or without 40 μM ALA for a specific period, which ranged from 5 to 60 min. Vero cells were then inoculated with the mixture. After 1 h, the inoculum was replaced by fresh medium. At 72 hpi, cells were collected for ZIKV E mRNA detection by RT-PCR, as well as ZIKV E protein detection by western blotting assay. ALA treatment caused a 99% reduction rate of E mRNA level, even if the mixture was incubated for only 5 min (Fig. 3A). Similarly, ZIKV E protein expression also decreased quickly as shown in Fig. 3C and D. Therefore, we may conclude that ALA inhibits ZIKV replication quickly.

To clarify which stages of ZIKV lifecycle was blocked by ALA, we firstly performed the virus binding test. Vero cells were seeded in 6-well plates and cultivated overnight. After that, diluted ALA was incubated with ZIKV at 37°C for 10 min before inoculation to the cell culture. Epigallocatechin gallate (EGCG) was adopted as a positive control. The supernatant was discarded after 1 h of incubation at 4°C, which allowed virus binding rather than internalization, and cells were then washed five times with PBS. Cellular RNA was isolated, followed by RT-qPCR test for quantification of ZIKV mRNA copies. The mRNA levels were significantly reduced when cells were treated with 5–50 μM ALA (Fig. 4A) and 10 μM EGCG (data not shown). These results indicate that ALA inhibits ZIKV binding to Vero cells. We then tested the anti-adsorption activity of ALA. Similar to the binding test, Vero cells were inoculated with ALA-ZIKV mixture and incubated at 4°C for 1 h. Cells were then washed with PBS or treated with pronase, and then re-fed with fresh medium. Following 72 h of incubation, cellular RNA was isolated and RT-PCR test was conducted for quantification of ZIKV E and NS1 mRNA copies. As shown in Fig. 4B–C, in PBS washed cells, ALA at 5–50 μM remarkably prevented ZIKV infection, indicating that ALA inhibits ZIKV adsorption to Vero cells. While pronase was used to remove the cell-bound but uninternalized virus particles, similar results were obtained (Fig. S2A–S2B). Finally, we performed the anti-entry experiment. Vero cells were inoculated with ZIKV and incubated at 4°C for 1 h. The supernatant was discarded. Cells were then washed with PBS and exposed to fresh medium with ALA at different concentrations. After 1 h of incubation at 37°C, the culture was replenished with fresh medium. Following 72 hpi incubation, cellular RNA was isolated and RT-PCR test was conducted for quantification of ZIKV E and NS1 mRNA copies. As shown in Fig. 4D–E, ALA at 15–50 μM remarkably prevented ZIKV infection, indicating that ALA inhibits ZIKV entry into Vero cells. In conclusion, ALA interrupts the early stages in ZIKV replication cycle, including virus binding, adsorption and entry.

Because ALA interrupts the early stages of ZIKV life cycle, we suppose that it probably targets viral membrane and destroys membrane integrity of the virion. We performed RNase digestion assay using micrococcal nuclease to degrade viral RNA in case of an incomplete envelope. As shown in Fig. 5A, 5–50 μM ALA substantially suppressed ZIKV mRNA loads, indicating a dose-dependent destruction of viral membrane integrity. The positive control (1% Triton) also destroyed the membrane considerably, resulting in a complete RNA degradation by micrococcal nuclease.

We also explored the activities of ALA against DENV, HSV-1, influenza virus and SARS-CoV-2. To investigate whether ALA inhibited DENV infection, we infected BHK21 cells with the pre-incubated ALA-DENV-2 mixture. Following 1 h of incubation at 37°C, the supernatant was replaced with fresh medium. After 48 h of incubation, intracellular protein and RNA were extracted. The protein and mRNA levels of DENV NS5 were determined using western blotting and RT-PCR methods, respectively. As shown in Fig. 6A–B, 15–50 μM ALA prominently reduced the expression of DENV NS5 protein. Similarly, 5–50 μM ALA remarkably reduced transcription of DENV NS5 gene (Fig. 6C). These findings indicate that ALA is an effective inhibitor of DENV-2 infection. To investigate whether ALA inhibited HSV infection, we infected Vero cells with the pre-incubated ALA-HSV-1 mixture. Following 1 h of incubation at 37°C, the supernatant was replaced with fresh medium. After 24 h of incubation, intracellular protein was extracted and envelope glycoprotein D (gD) was examined using western blotting method. As shown in Fig. 6D and 2.5–10 μM ALA substantially decreased the expression of HSV gD. To investigate whether ALA inhibited influenza virus infection, we mixed influenza virus A/WSN/1933 (H1N1) with different concentrations of ALA for 10 min at room temperature. MDCK cells were then inoculated with the mixture. After 1 h of incubation at 37°C, the supernatant was replenished with fresh medium containing TPCK and without FBS. Following 48 h of incubation, MTT test was applied to estimate the antiviral activity of ALA. As displayed in Fig. 6E, 12.5–100 μM ALA exerted significant effect on H1N1 infection. To investigate whether ALA inhibited SARS-CoV-2 infection, we pre-incubated SARS-CoV-2 PsV with serial dilutions of ALA for 30 min at room temperature. Following that, 293T-ACE2 cells were inoculated with the mixture and incubated at 37°C for 12 h. After replacing the supernatant with fresh medium, cells were incubated for another 48 h. Viral infection was determined using the luciferase assay. As shown in Fig. 6F, ALA significantly inhibited SARS-CoV-2 infection, as well as the positive control chloroquine. PNL4/3 blank vector was used as a negative control and little fluorescence was detected (Fig. S4).

Here, our group confirmed for the first time at the mRNA and protein levels that ALA has a dose-dependent antiviral activity against ZIKV. ALA was effective even when its incubation with ZIKV only lasted for 5 min, preventing virus entry by targeting both viruses and cells. Time-of-addition assay revealed that ALA interrupts binding, adsorption, and entry stages of ZIKV replication cycle. It is probably owing to the disruption of viral membrane. Notably, ALA also shows broad-spectrum antiviral effects against DENV, HSV, influenza virus and SARS-CoV-2 infections. Our study revealed a promising ZIKV inactivator and entry inhibitor. During the early stages of ZIKV replication cycle, ALA efficiently inhibits viral binding and adsorption, whereas its activity against viral entry was not satisfactory. These findings may be attributed to the different experimental procedures. For the binding and adsorption assays, ALA was pre-incubated with ZIKV for 1 h, but it was mixed with the cells 1 h after ZIKV inoculation in the entry assay. Throughout the life cycle of a flavivirus, vRNA translation occurs within 1–5 hpi, vRNA is synthesized at ≥ 5 hpi, and the progeny virus is released at ≥ 5 hpi (Chambers et al., 1990; Chen et al., 2020). Thus, when cells were inoculated with ZIKV before ALA treatment, part of vRNA had been translated into polyproteins, ALA didn’t work on this virus population. ALA is a lipophilic molecule, which could interfere with host cell receptor function by altering envelope dynamics, and then destabilize envelope proteins and lipids of the viral membrane (Mounce et al., 2017; Wang and Xia, 2019). Resultantly, structural integrity of the viral membrane is disrupted by ALA, and then the genomic RNA of ZIKV is released and degraded by micrococcal nuclease. As a key step for virus infection, the receptors or receptor cofactors on host cell surface are recognized by membrane proteins on the surface of virus particles, and then viral entry is triggered. Up till now, several receptors or receptor cofactors, such as DC-SIGN, TIM-1, TAM (Axl, Tyro 3, Mertk), and Hsp 70, have been found to increase ZIKV infectivity (Lee et al., 2021; Meertens et al., 2017; Nowakowski et al., 2016; Pujhari and Brustolin, 2019). However, the TAM family is reported not to be an essential element for ZIKV infection in mice (Hastings et al., 2017; Li et al., 2017; Wang et al., 2017). ALA also targets the cells to inhibit ZIKV infection (Fig. 3B), rather than other virus infection (Fig. S3), suggesting maybe ZIKV binds to cells via unique recepters. But which receptors are bound by ALA needs to be further explored.

Besides its antiviral effect against ZIKV, ALA also dose-dependently inhibited DENV infection. Thus, ALA may be an ideal candidate for the treatment of ZIKV and DENV co-infection, which is common in regions where Aedes aegypti is abundant. At the same time, the antibody-dependent enhancement (ADE) effect, suggesting that pre-existing DENV antibody may be cross-reactive and enhance ZIKV infection, has raised remarkable concern (Dai et al., 2021; Richner et al., 2017). ALA, as a bifunctional antiviral agent of these two viruses, may decrease the ADE effect to a certain extent. It will be noteworthy to explore the effects of ALA against other flaviviruses infection in the future. In our study, ALA also inhibited SARS-CoV-2 infection. This result is consistent with the previous research (Mounce et al., 2017) although their effective concentrations were higher than ours. They elucidated the mechanism of blocking SARS-CoV-2 entry, which could also be explained by disrupting membrane integrity of the virions. However, it needs to be confirmed by further exploration. In addition, ALA has been developed as a health-care product and dietary supplement, mainly for seniors, children, and pregnant women. Therefore, its safety and availability offers advantages over other antivirals. Altogether, the capability of ALA to prevent viral replication suggests that this molecule and its derivatives are ideal candidates for the development of broad-spectrum antivirals.
